brief.alert_summary: Ambulatory BP monitor shows persistent nocturnal hypertension: 164/98 mmHg at 2026-05-13T02:15Z, with prior nocturnal systolic readings of 160 and 158 mmHg over the preceding 48 hours. This is above the supplied nighttime systolic threshold and higher than prior daytime home values documented on amlodipine.
brief.patient_snapshot: 63-year-old man with active essential hypertension, taking amlodipine 10 mg daily. Recent clinic documentation noted daytime home readings generally 128-138/76-86 on amlodipine. Creatinine was normal in March 2026. He reports taking amlodipine every morning and has recently had disrupted sleep from knee pain, with concern that blood pressure is mainly high overnight.

contextual_analysis.text: The current RPM reading shows nocturnal BP 164/98 mmHg with good signal quality, and the trend suggests sustained nocturnal systolic elevation over 48 hours (160, 158, 164).
Prior EHR note documented daytime home readings generally 128-138/76-86 on amlodipine, suggesting the recent elevation is more pronounced at night than in prior daytime monitoring.
Current management appears to be amlodipine 10 mg once daily, with the patient reporting morning adherence. No other antihypertensive agents are listed in the supplied record.

The patient reports knee pain disrupting sleep for four nights and started ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily five days ago, both of which may be relevant to the timing and persistence of elevated nighttime BP.

risk_assessment.text: Persistent nocturnal BP elevation may indicate inadequate control during the sleep period and warrants timely clinical review.
Recent ibuprofen use could be contributing to higher BP and should be reviewed in context of hypertension management.
Disrupted sleep from pain may be associated with elevated nocturnal readings and should be assessed alongside adherence and medication timing.
No immediate crisis features are provided, and the alert does not include severe symptoms or unstable vital signs beyond the elevated BP values.

recommended_actions.action: Prompt clinician review of the nocturnal BP trend and current antihypertensive plan.
recommended_actions.rationale: The monitor shows sustained nighttime systolic elevation above threshold despite reported morning amlodipine use.

recommended_actions.action: Review medication timing, adherence, and any recent changes in antihypertensive regimen.
recommended_actions.rationale: Clarifying dosing time and adherence may help explain the nocturnal pattern and guide management.

recommended_actions.action: Ask about symptoms and contributors, including headache, chest pain, shortness of breath, sleep disruption, pain, and recent NSAID use.
recommended_actions.rationale: These factors can affect BP control and determine whether further urgent assessment is needed.

recommended_actions.action: Consider advising the patient to avoid further OTC NSAID use until reviewed, if clinically appropriate.
